coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a sensitive colorimetric nanosensor for determination of cysteine in human serum and urine samples based on magnetic-sulfur, nitrogen graphene quantum dots as a selective platform and Au nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Afsharipour, Roya
Dadfarnia, Shayessteh
Haji Shabani, Ali Mohammad
Kazemi, Elahe
Pedrini, Alessandro
Verucchi, Roberto
coredata.dc:description: A novel colorimetric nanosensor is reported for the selective and sensitive determination of cysteine using magnetic-sulfur, nitrogen graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs), and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). Thus, S, N-GQDs was firstly immobilized on Fe3O4 nanoparticles through its magnetization in the presence of Fe3+ in the alkali solution. The prepared Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs were dispersed in cysteine solution resulting in its quick adsorption on the surface of the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs through hydrogen bonding interaction. Then, Au NPs solution was added to this mixture that after a short time, the color of Au NPs changed from red to blue, the intensity of surface plasmon resonance peak of Au NPs at 530 nm decreased, and a new peak at a higher wavelength of 680 nm appeared. The effective parameters on cysteine quantification were optimized via response surface methodology using the central composite design. Under optimum conditions, the absorbance ratio (A680/A530) has a linear proportionality with cysteine concentration in the range of 0.04–1.20 μmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.009 μmol L−1. The fabrication of the reported nanosensor is simple, fast, and is capable of efficient quantification of ultra traces of cysteine in human serum and urine real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cysteine
Colorimetric nanosensor
Fe3O4/S
N-GQDs
Au NPs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Strategy and theoretical aspect of Cys sensing by the designed system
Comparison of designed nanosensor with other methods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical signal versus time, (a) in the absence, and, (b) in the presence of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs.
Schematic representation of Cys determination-induced aggregation of Au NPs in the presence of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs-Cys as a colorimetric nanosensor.
(a) S, N-GQDs TEM image, (b) FESEM image of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs, (c) XRD pattern of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs, (d) VSM curve of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs.

XPS analysis of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs. a) wide range spectrum; background-subtracted spectra of S2p (b), N1s (c), and C1s (d) core levels, with the description of single components. It is worth noting that S2p is composed of four peaks, each of them being an unresolved 2p1/2-2p3/2 double peak with the spin-orbit splitting of 1.18 eV.
3-D plots for the Cys determination. (a) pH and interaction time, (b) pH and concentration of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au NPs in the presence of various concentrations of Cys from 0.0 to 1.20 μM, (b) The linear relationship between the analytical signal and concentrations of Cys (0.04–1.20 μM).
Parameters and their corresponding levels in central composite design with experimental results for determination of Cys.
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) for central composite design.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Test for comparison of the model variance with residual (error) variance.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: ANOVA analysis statistical outputs for different models.
Determination of Cys in real samples (n = 3).
Comparison of the developed method with other nanosensors for the determination of Cys.

row.entry: Bovine serum albumin and fetal bovine serum
Usage a typical excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) dye

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a sensitive colorimetric nanosensor for determination of cysteine in human serum and urine samples based on magnetic-sulfur, nitrogen graphene quantum dots as a selective platform and Au nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Yazd University, Yazd, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environmental and Bio-Analytical Laboratories, Department of Chemistry, Sharif University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Life Science and Environmental Sustainability, University of Parma, Parco Area Delle Scienze 17/A, 43124 Parma, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Life Science and Environmental Sustainability, University of Parma, Parco Area delle Scienze 17/A, 43124 Parma, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: IMEM-CNR, Institute of Materials for Electronics and Magnetism, Trento Unit C/o Fondazione Bruno Kessler, Via Alla Cascata 56/C, Povo, Trento IT-38123, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials for Electronics and Magnetism
Trento Unit C/o Fondazione Bruno Kessler

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: IMEM-CNR, Institute of Materials for Electronics and Magnetism, Trento unit c/o Fondazione Bruno Kessler, Via alla Cascata 56/C, Povo, Trento IT-38123, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel colorimetric nanosensor is reported for the selective and sensitive determination of cysteine using magnetic-sulfur, nitrogen graphene quantum dots (Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs), and gold nanoparticles (Au NPs). Thus, S, N-GQDs was firstly immobilized on Fe3O4 nanoparticles through its magnetization in the presence of Fe3+ in the alkali solution. The prepared Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs were dispersed in cysteine solution resulting in its quick adsorption on the surface of the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs through hydrogen bonding interaction. Then, Au NPs solution was added to this mixture that after a short time, the color of Au NPs changed from red to blue, the intensity of surface plasmon resonance peak of Au NPs at 530 nm decreased, and a new peak at a higher wavelength of 680 nm appeared. The effective parameters on cysteine quantification were optimized via response surface methodology using the central composite design. Under optimum conditions, the absorbance ratio (A680/A530) has a linear proportionality with cysteine concentration in the range of 0.04–1.20 μmol L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.009 μmol L−1. The fabrication of the reported nanosensor is simple, fast, and is capable of efficient quantification of ultra traces of cysteine in human serum and urine real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (Cys) is a thiol-containing amino acid that plays an important role in different biological processes including the synthesis of proteins and peptides, metabolism, and neuronal tissues [1–3]. Abnormal levels of cysteine have been associated with various diseases such as skin and liver diseases, lethargy, edema, weakness, hair depigmentation, and muscle loss [2,3]. Thus, the development of a sensitive, cost-effective, and simple method for quantification of cysteine levels in biological fluids in the presence of other thiol compounds is an important task for the promotion of human health. Several analytical approaches have been reported for the detection and determination of Cys including high-performance liquid chromatography [4], electrochemical techniques [5], chemiluminescence [6], spectrofluorimetric and colorimetric methods [7–12]. Most of these methods either required a sample preparation step or the use of sophisticated instruments. Among these techniques, colorimetric nanosensors have higher attraction due to their facility, simplicity, low cost, rapidness, and the possibility of recognition with the naked eye. In 2015, Bhamore Karuna and coworkers designed a colorimetric nanosensor based on the synthesis of gold nanoparticles for selective determination of Cys and glutathione. This nanoparticle was prepared by utilizing ascorbic acid as the reducing and capping agent [13]. In another study by Yu et al. reported a friendly environmental method for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine-1-oxyl-oxidized cellulose nanofibrils as the reducing agent. The silver nanoparticles were coated on the surface of cellulose nanofibrils and the resulted suspension was used for the determination of Cys [14].

The use of nanomaterials in the fabrication of fluorescent and colorimetric sensor is in progress due to their great electronic and optical advantages [15–21]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial with excellent photoluminescence properties, stability, the possibility of functionalization of its surface groups, as well as ease of preparation [22,23]. The GQDs synthesized by hydrothermal methods are highly soluble in water limiting their application as a catalyst, and sorptive materials. To overcome this problem, magnetic GQDs have been prepared by various researchers [24–26], however, most of their methods were multi-steps, required a stabilizer and reducing agents. In 2016, Shi and coworkers prepared a magnetic nitrogen graphene quantum dots by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis, without a reducing and stabilizing agent and applied it as a non-enzymatic catalyst for visual discriminating of phenylenediamine isomers [27]. Based on our literature consideration, there is no other report on the synthesis and application of magnetic GQDs by this method.

Gold nanoparticles achieved great attention in the colorimetric assays for the detection of important biological molecules [28,29], in vivo analysis [30], and living-cell imaging [31]. The significant characteristic of gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) which make them a good candidate for the colorimetric probe in the analysis of biological samples is their localized surface plasmon resonance properties [8]. The bases of these colorimetric assessments are the aggregation or morphology changes of Au NPs resulting in a change in their optical and plasmonic properties [32]. Although these methods are appropriate for the determination of biological molecules containing –SH group, they are not able to discriminate biomolecules with a similar functional structure [33–35]. Thus, the development of a selective, facile, and sensitive colorimetric method based on Au NPs localized surface plasmon resonance properties for the determination of molecules with similar functional groups is attractive.

Due to the importance of cysteine in discrimination of various diseases, this study aims to design an optical sensitive nanosensor for direct determination of trace amount of cysteine in real samples including human serum and urine. In this regard, the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs was synthesized in a one-pot manner by a hydrothermal method by the addition of Fe3+ to S, N-GQDs solution under alkaline conditions, without the addition of stabilizer and reducing agent. Then, a novel strategy was designed for selective determination of trace amount of cysteine using Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs, and Au NPs as a colorimetric nanosensor. The central composite design (CCD) was used to optimize the parameters affecting the assay of Cys by the designed method. The capability of the designed method in the selective quantification of Cys in real samples was studied.

Citric acid, thiourea, FeCl3, FeCl2·4H2O, l-cysteine, l-glutathione, glycine, L-serin, phenylalanine, and histidine were purchased from Merck Company (Darmstadt, Germany). All other reagents were analytical purity grade and utilized directly without any purification. A standard solution of Cys (50.0 μmol L−1) was freshly prepared and was kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator. Also, all aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water.

The absorption spectra were recorded in the range of 200–600 nm by the UV–Vis spectrophotometer model SPECORD 250 (Analytik Jena AG, Germany). The pH adjustment was done by an inolab pH meter (model 7110, Germany). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded by a Bruker Vector 22 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker Equinox 55, Germany) using KBr pellets. A diffractometer model X’ Pert Pro (Panalytical, Netherlands) was used to obtain the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern. A Zeiss-EM10C electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 100 kV (Zeiss, Germany) was used for obtaining the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. A ZEISS Sigma VP FESEM with a mapping detector (ZEISS, Germany) was used for recording the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been carried out in an Ultra-High-Vacuum (UHV) system, equipped with a non-monochromatized X-ray source (Mg Kα photon at 1253.6 eV) and a VSW HA100 hemispherical analyzer, leading to a total energy resolution of 0.86 eV. The binding energy (BE) scale of XPS spectra was calibrated by using the Au 4f peak at 84.0 eV as a reference. The core level analysis has been performed by Voigt line-shape deconvolution after background subtraction of a Shirley function.

S, N-GQDs was synthesized according to the previously reported procedure with some modifications [33]. For this purpose, citric acid as the carbon source (1.26 g) and thiourea (1.38 g) as the nitrogen and sulfur source were dissolved into 30.0 mL of distilled water in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. Then, the autoclave was sealed and heated at 190 °C for 8.0 h in an oven. Finally, the obtained S, N-GQDs solution was kept in a dark container and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator.

For the Fe3O4/N-GQDs synthesis, 18.0 mL of S, N-GQDs solution was transferred in a beaker containing 2.4 mL sodium hydroxide solution (1.0 mol L−1). Then, 7.5 mL FeCl3 solution (2.0 mol L−1) was slowly added to the mixture and sonicated for 10.0 min at room temperature. The resulted mixture was sealed in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, and heated at 190 °C for 8.0 h. The final solid product was separated ad hold by an external magnet, rinsed several times with distilled water, and finally dried at 50 °C in an oven. The synthesis process is shown schematically in Fig. S1.

The synthesis of Au NPs with 30 nm size was performed by the reduction of HAuCl4 with sodium citrate according to the previous procedure [36]. Thus, 150.0 mL of sodium citrate solution (2.2 mM) was transferred into a three-necked round bottom flask (250.0 mL) and heated under reflux conditions with continuous vigorous stirring. At the commencement of the boiling of sodium citrate, 1.0 mL of HAuCl4 solution (25.0 mM) was added and the mixture was heated for further 10.0 min. In this process, the color of the mixture was first altered from yellow to bluish-gray and after a short time to light pink confirming the right formation of Au seeds. After this stage, the mixture was cooled down to 90 °C, followed by sequential injection of 1 mL of sodium citrate (60.0 mM) and 1 mL of a HAuCl4 solution (25.0 mM) with a time delay of 2.0 min, the mixture was stirred continuously for 30 min, and Au NPs with the average size of about 30 nm was obtained. At last, the prepared mixture was cooled at room temperature and stored in a refrigerator.

For determination of Cys, 10.0 μL of the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs solution (2.2 mg mL−1), 940.0 μL standard or sample solution of Cys along with 250.0 μL of Britton-Robinson (0.2 M, pH 7.0) were transferred into a 1.5 mL Eppendorf tube, 300.0 μL of Au NPs solution was added to it and the mixture was vortexed for 1.0 min, followed by incubation for 3.0 min at room temperature. Then, the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs were collected by the aim of an external magnet, the supernatant solution was transferred to a spectrophotometric cell and its absorption spectrum was recorded in the range of 400–800 nm. The absorption ratio (A680/A530) was used as the analytical signal in the Cys quantification.

Serum samples (1.0 mL) were provided from volunteers (Razi laboratory of Yazd) and were kept frozen at −20 °C before analysis. Each sample was thawed, acetonitrile (0.8 mL) was added, mixed for 45 s, and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the protein residue. Then, the supernatant was properly diluted with deionized water to the linear range of the developed method and treated according to the given procedure (section 2.5).

Urine sample (from volunteers in our laboratory) was centrifuged at 500 rpm for 10 min and the white lipid was settled down. After filtration of supernatant through a 0.45 μm filter, its pH was adjusted and subjected to the given procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Strategy and theoretical aspect of Cys sensing by the designed system
ce:section.ce:para: In the absence of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs the reaction between Cys and Au NPs was slow, and the analytical signal reaches its maximum after 1 h (Fig. 1 a) whereas with Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs the maximum signal was obtained after 3.0 min (Fig. 1b). Furthermore, in the absence of Cys no aggregation of Au NPs or change in solution color was observed. This observation can be explained on the basis that Cys is adsorbed on Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs with the high surface area through hydrogen bonding, and after the addition of Au NPs, the gold particles interact quickly with the immobilized Cys through the formation of the specific Au–S bonds. This resulted in the release of the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs particles followed by the change in solution color and enhancement of the analytical signal in a short time of 3 min, as schematically shown in Fig. 2 .

FT-IR spectra were utilized to confirm the main functional groups on the surface of S, N GQDs, and Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs. As exhibited in Figs. S1a and b, the bands at 3000-3500 cm−1 are due to stretching vibration of O–H and N–H respectively, illustrating the existence of amino and hydroxyl groups on the surface of S, N-GQDs, and Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs. The bands that appeared in both spectra at 1071 cm−1 and 630 cm−1 are related to CS, and C–S, respectively, and the peaks at 1710 and 1611 cm−1 are due to the vibration bands of CO in COOH and CONH, respectively. It can be seen in Fig. S2b, the absorption band of O–H group was decreased in the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs spectrum. The appearance of a band at 590 cm−1 in Fig. S2b confirms the right magnetization of S, N-GQDs. The decrease in the intensity of –OH group at 3401 cm −1 in Fig. S2b in comparison to Fig. S2a can be attributed to the oxidization of the surface –OH groups of S, N-GQDs into –COOH groups by Fe3+. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S2c, in the presence of Cys the absorption peak of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs at 1710 cm−1 is broadened and shifted to 1611 cm−1 confirming the formation of the hydrogen bond between their functional groups and immobilization of Cys.

The morphology and the particle size of the S, N-GQDs was further evaluated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM image shows that the synthesized S, N-GQDs are nearly spherical in the shape with an average size of 3–5 nm (Fig. 3 a). The FESEM consideration of the size of the prepared Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs indicates a size distribution of about 15–25 nm with a globular morphology (Fig. 3b).

The XRD patterns of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs were compared with the bare Fe3O4 prepared by the method reported in the literature [37]. As demonstrated in Fig. 3c, the diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 30.4, 34.4, 35.8, 43.4, 54.0, 57.4, 61.4, 71.9 and 76.6° are related to the presence of pure cubic spinel crystal of Fe3O4, while the diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 28.6, 40.9, 64.1° can be ascribed to the presence of S, N-GQDs in the Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs structure. The increase in the intensity of the diffraction peaks of Fe3O4 in Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs in comparison to the bare Fe3O4 (Fig. S3) can be related to an increase in crystallinity of Fe3O4 as a result of synthesizing of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs at a higher temperature.

XPS analysis (see wide range spectrum in Fig. 4 a) has shown the presence of C, O, S, N, and Fe elements, with the atomic percentage of 54.70, 27.14, 9.40, 4.73, and 4.03% respectively, in good agreement with previous studies [38]. Fe 2p core level analysis (see Fig. S4a), based on the approach of Gupta and Sen for deconvolution of multiplet structure [39,40], shows Fe2p3/2 BEs for main Fe2+ and Fe3+ peaks at 709.88 and 711.80 eV, with a Fe2+/Fe3+ ratio of 1.8, i.e. typical of Fe3O4 as confirmed by a Fe/O atomic percentage ratio of 0.73 and the absence of a satellite at about 720 eV [39,40]. The peak at 707.53 eV is related to interaction with S, giving rise to a corresponding component in the S2p spectrum at 161.49 eV [41], while the unresolved region at ~714 eV is due to the formation of Fe–S–O compounds, as confirmed by the S2p peak (Fig. 4b) at 167.30 eV [39,40,42]. S2p core level is composed of other two features at 163.15 and 164.47 eV, due to C–S–C [43] and C–S–S–C disulfide groups [44]. N1s core level (Fig. 4c) has a component related to NH2 at 399.08 eV [43] and a broad peak at 401.61 eV, probably due to several species such as Fe–N [45], graphitic N [46], and CNO groups [47]. C1s core level main component at 284.99 eV is due to the C–C network (see Fig. 4d), with the presence of other peaks at 285.72, 286.54, 287.82, and 289.06 eV resulting from C–S bonds [48], C–N/C–O [M], CO and COOH/CONH [43] respectively. The weak feature at 291.30 eV is attributed to the π-π* shake-up process, typical of π-conjugated systems [43,49]. O1s peak analysis (see Fig. S4b) confirms the presence of two typical Fe3O4 components at 530.43 and 531.33 eV [39,40], as well as CO and C–O species at 531.90 and 533.92 eV [50]. XPS data (see Table S1 for further details) confirm the synthesis of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs, with magnetite having good chemical properties and several interaction paths with the GQDs, mainly involving sulfur-based compounds.

The magnetic property of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs was investigated by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) analysis. It can be seen in Fig. 3d that the magnetic hysteresis loop is reversible, confirming the permanent magnetic property of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs synthesized at the relatively high temperature of 190 °C in the autoclave. Thus, Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs will strongly respond to the external magnetic field.

Response surface methodology (RSM) was utilized for the experimental design and investigating the influence of variables on the designed system to obtain the best conditions for the determination of Cys. The central composite design (CCD) was used for evaluation of the effect of three variables of pH (X
1 ), the concentration of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs (X
2 ) and interaction time between Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs/Cys and Au NPs (X
3 ) on the determination of Cys. To decrease the uncontrollable effects, each factor was investigated at five levels (±α, ± 1, 0). Thus, based on full class in CCD using the equation of N = 2k + 2k + N0 (where, N is the runs total number, k is the parameters number and N0 is the central point's number), 20 experiments including eight factorial points (2k), six axial points (2k) and six central points (N0) were performed (Table 1 ). The experimental error and the reproducibility of the analysis were determined using the central points of CCD [51]. The estimated results were fitted to a second-order polynomial model as presented below:
Y =
b 0
+
∑
i = 1
n
b i
X i
+
∑
i = 1
n
b
i i
X i 2
+
∑
i = 1
n − 1
∑
j = i + 1
n
b
i j
X i
X j
+
e i

In this equation, Y is the predicted response; X
i and X
j are independent variables; n is defined as the number of variables (n = 3); b
0 is the intercept, b
i is the linear, bii is quadratic, and b
ij is the interaction coefficients; and e
i is the residual error [52,53]. Then, the analysis of variance (ANOVA) was employed to calculate the importance of independent parameters and their interactions at the 95% confidence level. Furthermore, the fitness of the model with each parameter was statistically examined by calculating the Fisher test value (F-value) at the p-value < 0.05. The quality of the proposed polynomial was checked through the evaluation of calculated values for R 2, adjusted R 2, predicted R 2, lack of fit (LOF > 0.05), and adequate precision.

According to ANOVA analysis (Table 2 ), among the linear, two-factor interaction and quadratic model tested the quadratic model with the following equation, lowest p-value as well as R 2, adjusted R 2, and predicted R 2 closer to one was fitted better to the experimental results (Table 3 ).
R = 1.80 − 0.054
X 1
+ 0.029
X 2
+ 0.05
X 3
+ 0.016
X 1
X 2
− 0.024
X 1
X 3
− 0.17
X 1 2
− 0.025
X 2 2
− 0.023
X 3 2
The plot of the results of experimental data of the actual values against the predicted one shown in Fig. S5, demonstrate a good fit between the results and the quadratic polynomial model.

The 3D response surface graphs were employed for considering the interaction between parameters and obtaining the optimum value for each parameter. The pH of the solution may be an important factor affecting the Cys interactions as it contains three pH-dependent functional groups of –SH, COOH, and NH2. Thus, the effect of pH in the range of 5.0–9.0, on the determination of Cys by the designed method was examined. According to the result of Fig. 5 a, and b, the maximum response is achieved at a pH of 7.0. A decrease in responses observed in the alkaline solution can be related to the decrease in adsorption of Cys on the surface Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs due to the deprotonation of its functional groups, resulting in the decrease in the speed of Cys interaction with Au NPs. The concentration of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs can also play a significant role in the determination process of Cys. According to Fig. 5b, Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs concentration of 2.2 mg mL−1 was sufficient for complete interaction with Cys which was selected as the optimum value for further analysis. The reaction time between the Cys and Au NPs is a factor affecting the speed and sensitivity of the procedure. As shown in Fig. 5a, the response is enhanced by increasing the interaction time up to 3.0 min and remained constant by a further increase in time indicating the achievement of equilibrium in the interaction of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs/Cys with Au NPs. Thus, 3.0 min was chosen as an optimum value for complete interaction between Au NPs and Cys.

The figure of merits of determination of Cys by the designed nanosensor was evaluated under the optimum conditions. As presented in Fig. 6 b, the absorbance ratio (A680/A530) possess a good linear relationship with concentrations of Cys in the range of 0.04–1.20 μmol L−1 with the regression equation of S = 0.6511C + 0.1850 (where S is the absorbance ratio (A680/A530) and C is the concentration of Cys (μmol L−1) in solution) and the coefficient of determination of 0.9982. The detection and quantification limits, calculated based on the three and ten times of the blank solution standard deviation divided by the slope of the calibration curve, were found to be 0.009 and 0.03 μmol L−1, respectively. The precision of the developed nanosensor was determined via the relative standard deviation (RSD) by examining six replicate determinations of the solutions containing Cys at the concentration level of 0.4 μmol L−1 and the RSDs of 2.1 and 4.5% were obtained for intra-day and inter-day experiments.

The effect of some essential amino acids on the determination of Cys by the designed system was studied. As illustrated in Fig. S6a, under optimized conditions, the amino acids including glycine, L-serin, phenylalanine, asparagine, and histidine at a concentration of 100.0 μmol L−1, and glutathione at 40.0 μmol L−1 and metal ions (Fe2+, Hg2+, Cu2+, and Pb2+ at the concentration level of 100.0 μmol L−1) have no important influence on the absorbance ratio (A680/A530) and determination of Cys at 0.4 μmol L−1 level. The selectivity of the designed sensor was also investigated in the solution of the amino acids or metal ions in the presence of Cys, but no considerable change was not observed in analytical signals (Fig. S6b). The selectivity of the designed system for Cys in comparison to glutathione, an amino acid with thiol group can be related to the difference in conformation and interaction of thiol groups in Cys and glutathione with Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs, and Au NPs. The adsorbed Cys can quickly interact with Au NPs and induces its aggregation, leading to the color change from red to blue, whereas the interaction of bulky amino acid of glutathione with Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs, and Au NPs is limited.

The human serum and urine samples were properly diluted to get into the calibration range, and the amount of Cys in them was measured by the designed procedure. For evaluation of the accuracy of the method, each sample was spiked with two concentrations of Cys and the recoveries were determined. The results (Table 4 ) indicated the amounts of recoveries in the spiked samples were in the range of 96.0–103.0% indicating the success of the colorimetric nanosensor for the quantitative determination of Cys in the samples types studied. The accuracy was also further verified by independent analysis [4] and the results are provided in Table 4. As realized at a 95% confidence level there is no significant difference between the results of the two methods. Thus, the method has acceptable accuracy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of designed nanosensor with other methods
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical characteristics of the designed nanosensor were compared with the recently reported nanosensors for the determination of Cys (Table 5 ). The result confirmed that the designed method is faster, its dynamic range is comparable and with one exception, Ref. 44 and 13, their limits of detection are much lower than other ones. It should be noted that in comparison to Ref. 39 the designed nanosensor is simpler and required less labor work.

In this study, a novel strategy was designed for the selective determination of trace amounts of cysteine using Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs, and Au NPs as a colorimetric nanosensor. The Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs were synthesized in a one-pot manner by a hydrothermal method without the addition of stabilizer and reducing agent. The effects of important parameters on the determination process were optimized by surface response methodology (RMS) using central composite design (CCD).

The adsorption of Cys on the surface of Fe3O4/S, N-GQDs increases the speed of aggregation of Au NPs and improves the selectivity of the nanosensor for its quantification. The fabricated nanosensor demonstrates good analytical performance for the quantification of Cys in biological samples with a high speed. The designed nanosensor is simple, facile, and effective for selective and sensitive determination of Cys in the samples containing other thiol biomolecules.

Roya Afsharipour: Data curation, Investigation, Original draft preparation, Shayessteh Dadfarnia and Ali Mohammad Haji Shabani: Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Elahe Kazemi: advisor, conceptualization, Alessandro Pedrini and Roberto Verucchi performed the XPS analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2020.122055.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multi-channel colorimetric and fluorescent probes for differentiating between cysteine and glutathione/homocysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Song, T. Jia, W. Lu, N. Jia, W. Zhang, J. Qian, Multi-channel colorimetric and fluorescent probes for differentiating between cysteine and glutathione/homocysteine. Org. Biomol. Chem. 12 (2014) 8422-8427.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, Y. Guo, R.M. Strongin, Conjugate addition/cyclization sequence enables selective and simultaneous fluorescence detection of cysteine and homocysteine, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 50 (2011) 10690-10693.
